56.play: Beshrew your heart for sending me about

57.play: To catch my death with jaunting up and down.

58.play: JULIET: I’ faith, I am sorry that thou art not well.

59.play: Sweet, sweet, sweet nurse, tell me, what says my

61.play: NURSE: Your love says, like an honest gentleman, and a

62.play: courteous, and a kind, and a handsome, and, I

63.play: warrant, a virtuous—Where is your mother?

64.play: JULIET: Where is my mother? Why, she is within.

65.play: Where should she be? How oddly thou repliest:

66.play: “Your love says, like an honest gentleman,
notes: Your love says, &c.] DYCE. Is this speech slightly corrupted? or ought it to be printed as prose? [Vide S. WALKER (‘Crit.,’ vol. i, p. 21).] ED. ULR. The loquacity of the Nurse, her praise of Romeo’s looks, her hesitation in delivering his message, all are features to be found in Arthur Brooke’s poem. The very answer which Romeo gives the Nurse in the preceding scene—“she shall be shriv’d and married”—is word for word in Brooke. The latter also expressly states that Romeo had given gold to the Nurse.

69.play: Are you so hot? Marry, come up, I trow.

70.play: Is this the poultice for my aching bones?

72.play: JULIET: Here’s such a coil. Come, what says Romeo?
notes: coil] NARES. Noise, tumult, difficulty. Of very uncertain derivation. DYCE. Bustle, stir, tumult, turmoil. CLARKE. Sh. sometimes uses it to express what is signified in modern parlance by ‘fuss,’ ‘to-do.’

73.play: NURSE: Have you got leave to go to shrift today?

75.play: NURSE: Then hie you hence to Friar Lawrence’ cell.

76.play: There stays a husband to make you a wife.

77.play: Now comes the wanton blood up in your cheeks;

78.play: They’ll be in scarlet straight at any news.
notes: They’ll . . . . news] COLL. [‘Notes and Emend.’]. It was not ‘at any news’ that Juliet’s cheeks would be in scarlet, but at the particular and joyful tidings brought by the Nurse. ULR. The old reading yields no sense, and has been left unmolested by the edd. only because it is the Nurse who speaks. The correction of Collier’s (MS.), although it departs widely from the text, I unhesitatingly adopt. COLL. (ed. 2). We do not feel warranted in varying here from the ordinary text, although the emendation of the (MS.) has some plausibility. The question is, whether the Nurse means to make an allusion to Juliet's general habit of blushing 'at any news,' or whether she alludes to the scarlet that must be called up into the cheeks of the heroine by the particular intelligence she is to communicate. We think the former, because the Nurse has already told the most important and interesting part of her information. WHITE. The old text has an appropriate meaning and must stand. DYCE (ed. 2). Walker ('Crit.' vol. ii, p. 255) would read 'straight at my next news.' But according to Capell the original text is right: 'at such talk (of love and Romeo), any talk of that kind, says the speaker, 'tis their custom to put on "scarlet!"'—Notes, &c., vol. ii, P. iv, p. 12. KNTL. In the errata of a work printed in 1754 I met 'for my i. any.' I, however, read in preference, 'They will be straight in scarlet at my news.' Sc. VI.] STEEV. This was entirely new-formed after the first copy. [Sing. Knt. Coll. Huds. WHITE ('Introd.' p. 22). The traces of another hand than Sh.'s that have attracted my attention in (Q1) are not many, but they seem to me unmistakable. The first that I noticed is this entire Scene. It will be observed that the variations in (Q1) from the later version are of the most material nature; or rather that the whole Scene was rewritten, and but a few lines of the earlier version was retained. The change made upon the revision was not in all respects for the better. In the Friar's second speech the line, 'So light a foot ne'er hurts the trodden flower,' contains a daintier and more graceful, and therefore it would seem, a more appropriate, figure than, 'so light a foot Will ne'er wear out the everlasting flint,' although the three lines that follow these last have a fancy and a rhythm peculiarly Shakspearian; and again, in Juliet's reply, 'I am, if I be day, Come to my sun: shine forth, and make me fair,' has a touch of poetry more exquisite and more dramatic than is to be found in the rewritten scene, which, unmistakably Sh.'s, is not Sh.'s best. Of the re- writing, there is, to my mind, no question. But in the present instance, the original hand is not markedly inferior. The latter re- minder, lines 1026-1033, 1044, 1045, 1050, 1051 of (Q1) will, I think, hardly be attributed to Sh. at any period of his career, by readers of discrimination, who are well acquainted with his works and those of his elder contemporaries. They are too tame, feeble, and formal, both in rhythm and sense, to have ever been written by him for the stage.

79.play: Hie you to church. I must another way,

80.play: To fetch a ladder by the which your love

81.play: Must climb a bird’s nest soon when it is dark.

82.play: I am the drudge and toil in your delight,

83.play: But you shall bear the burden soon at night.

84.play: Go. I’ll to dinner. Hie you to the cell.
notes: to dinner] CLARKE. This, among many other passages in Sh., shows that twelve o'clock, or a little after, was the usual hour for dinner in his time.

85.play: JULIET: Hie to high fortune! Honest nurse, farewell.

2.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: So smile the heavens upon this holy act

3.play: That after-hours with sorrow chide us not.

4.play: ROMEO: Amen, amen. But come what sorrow can,

5.play: It cannot countervail the exchange of joy

6.play: That one short minute gives me in her sight.

7.play: Do thou but close our hands with holy words,
notes: Do thou but] COLERIDGE ('Lit. Rem.' vol. ii, p. 155). The precipitancy, which is the character of the play, is well marked in this short scene of waiting for Juliet's arrival.

8.play: Then love-devouring death do what he dare,

9.play: It is enough I may but call her mine.

10.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: These violent delights have violent ends
notes: These violent] MAL. So, in The Rape of Lucrece, 894: 'These violent vanities can never last.' [Sing.
violent ends] WALKER ('Vers.,' &c., p. 138) cites this line as an instance of the pronunciation of the same word in the same line at one time as a trisyllable and at another as a dissyllable.

11.play: And in their triumph die, like fire and powder,

12.play: Which, as they kiss, consume. The sweetest honey

13.play: Is loathsome in his own deliciousness

14.play: And in the taste confounds the appetite.

15.play: Therefore love moderately. Long love doth so.
notes: love moderately] VISCHER ('Aesthetik, oder Wissenschaft des Schönen,' 1857, vol. iii, p. 1124). In the view of Gervinus, who traces the tragical end throughout to the excess of violent passion, there is a species of tragedy which does not merely illustrate character, but which contains no truth of universal interest beyond the lesson that teaches the duty of moderation, a lesson which, as an abstract proposition of morality, can never be the basis of any great poetic work. Accordingly, Gervinus preaches moderation to Romeo—very properly, doubtless; Friar Lawrence does so too. But had Romeo minded the lesson, there would have been no impassioned youth, nor would Love have been represented in the Drama in all its power, its infinitude. At another time one may bethink himself that there are other things besides Love in the world,—reflection, duty,—but here and now the divinity of Love is the thing; this it is that is to be represented, an ideal passion. Even here there is, even here, a lesson that may be read by those who wish to read it, but for the present ignored, since, if it had been followed, there would have been no drama.

love] KNT. The ‘love’ thus drawn was the queen of love, for the ‘wind-swift Cupid’ had ‘wings.’ Sh. had here the same idea which suggested his own beautiful description at the close of the Venus and Adonis: ‘Thus weary of the world, away she hies, And yokes her silver doves; by whose swift aid, Their mistress, mounted, through the empty skies In her light chariot quickly is convey’d—’

16.play: Too swift arrives as tardy as too slow.

17.play: [Enter Juliet.]

18.play: Here comes the lady. O, so light a foot
notes: so light a foot] COLL. (ed. 2). Singer, following Steevens in this extract, and not having referred to (Q1), misquotes it in an accidentally material point, since a comparison shows that 'so light a foot,' as it stands in QqFf, had been misheard by the person who put together (Q1) (from shorthand or other notes), 'so light of foot.' Such was extremely likely to be the case. On any other account the variance is unimportant.

19.play: Will ne’er wear out the everlasting flint.

20.play: A lover may bestride the gossamers
notes: gossamer] STEEV. The long white filament which flies in the air in summer. [Dyce.] In Hannibal and Scipio, 1637, by Nabbes : 'Fine as Arachne's web, or gossamer [‘gosshemere.’ NARES], Whose curls, when garnished by their dressing, shew Like that spun ['thiune.' NARES] vapour when 'tis pearl'd with dew?' MAL. See Bullokar's English Expositor, 1616: 'Gossomor: Things that flye like cobwebs in the ayre.' NARES. From the French, gossampine, the cotton tree, which is from gossypium: properly, therefore, cotton wool. Also any light downy matter, such as the flying seeds of thistles and other plants. Now used not unfrequently to signify the long, floating cobwebs seen in fine weather in the air. HOLT WHITE. It is formed from the collected webs of flying spiders, and during calm weather in autumn, sometimes falls in amazing quantities. [Sing. SING. [Note on Lear, IV, vi, 49]. Some think it the down of plants; others the vapour arising from boggy or marshy ground in warm weather. The etymon of this word, which has puzzled the lexicographers, is said to be summer goose or summer gauze; hence 'gauze o' the summer,' its well-known name in the north. See Hora Momenta Cravena, or the Craven Dialect Exemplified, 1824, 8vo, p. 79.

21.play: That idles in the wanton summer air,

22.play: And yet not fall, so light is vanity.
notes: vanity] CLARKE. Here used for 'trivial pursuit,' 'vain delight.' The word was much employed in this sense by divines in Sh.'s time; and with much propriety is so put into the good old Friar's mouth.

23.play: JULIET: Good even to my ghostly confessor.

24.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Romeo shall thank thee, daughter, for us both.

25.play: JULIET: As much to him, else is his thanks too much.
notes: else is] CLARKE. Though 'thanks' was sometimes treated as a noun singular, we do not believe that Sh.'s ear would have allowed him to write 'As much to him, else is his thanks too much.'

26.play: ROMEO: Ah, Juliet, if the measure of thy joy

27.play: Be heaped like mine, and that thy skill be more

28.play: To blazon it, then sweeten with thy breath

29.play: This neighbor air, and let rich music’s tongue

30.play: Unfold the imagined happiness that both

31.play: Receive in either by this dear encounter.

32.play: JULIET: Conceit, more rich in matter than in words,
notes: conceit] MAL. It here means imagination. [Sing.] So in The Rape of Lucrece: '——which the conceited painter drew,' &c. [Sta. CRAIK [ 'Eng. of Sh.,' p. 135]. To conceit is another form of our still familiar to conceive. And the noun conceit, which survives with a limited meaning (the conception of a man by himself, which is so apt to be one of over-estimation), is also frequent in Sh. with the sense, nearly, of what we now call conception, in general. Sometimes it is used in a sense which might almost be said to be the opposite of what it now means; as when Juliet [in this passage] employs it as the term to denote her all-absorbing affection for Romeo; or as when Gratiano, in the Mer. of Ven., I, i, 90, speaks of a sort of men who 'do a wilful stillness entertain, With purpose to be dress'd in an opinion Of wisdom, gravity, profound conceit'— that is, deep thought. So, again, when Rosaline, in Love's Lah., II, i, 72, speaking of Biron, describes his 'fair tongue' as 'conceit's expositor,' all that she means is, that speech is the expounder of thought. The scriptural expression, still in familiar use, 'wise in his own conceit,' means merely wise in his own thought, or in his own eyes, as we are told in the margin the Hebrew literally signifies. In the New Testament, where we have 'in their own conceits,' the Greek is simply παρ' ἑαυτοῖς (in or with themselves).

33.play: Brags of his substance, not of ornament.

34.play: They are but beggars that can count their worth,
notes: beggars] STEEV. In Ant. and Cleo., I, i, 15: 'There's beggary in the love that can be reckoned.' [Sing.

35.play: But my true love is grown to such excess

36.play: I cannot sum up sum of half my wealth.
notes: sum up half] STA. The meaning seems plain enough,—'I cannot sum up the sum or total of half my wealth.'

37.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Come, come with me, and we will make short work,

38.play: For, by your leaves, you shall not stay alone

39.play: Till Holy Church incorporate two in one.

40.play: [They exit.]

2.play: BENVOLIO: I pray thee, good Mercutio, let’s retire.

3.play: The day is hot, the Capels are abroad,
notes: The day is hot] JOHNSON. It is observed that, in Italy, almost all assassinations are committed during the heat of summer. [Sing. Corn. Verp. Ital. REED. In Sir Thomas Smith's Commonwealth of England, 1583, b. ii, c. xix, p. 70: 'And commonly every yeere or each second yeere in the beginning of sommer or afterwards (for in the warme time people for the most part be more unruly), even in the calm time of peace, the prince with his counsell,' &c., &c. [Sing. Hal. Clarke.

4.play: And if we meet we shall not ’scape a brawl,
notes: And, if] WALKER ('Crit.' vol. ii, p. 153). And if [Read an if. ED.] is always, in the old plays, printed and if; indeed, an is uniformly written and, except in the form an 't were, which is, I think, made one word. [Foot-note by LETTSOM. Not uniformly. The folio, Midsum. N. D., I, ii, p. 147, col. 2, has ' and 'twere any Nightingale;' and so in 1 Hen. IV: II, i and ii, p. 53, col. 1, and p. 54, col. 1, 'And 'twere not as good a deede as,' &c.] Many of the errors to which this gave rise are, as yet, uncorrected.

5.play: For now, these hot days, is the mad blood stirring.

6.play: MERCUTIO: Thou art like one of these fellows that, when

7.play: he enters the confines of a tavern, claps me his

8.play: sword upon the table and says “God send me no

9.play: need of thee” and, by the operation of the second

10.play: cup, draws him on the drawer when indeed there is
notes: draws it] DEL. Draws is a neuter verb, and him, of QqFf, is the pleonastic Dativus ethicus.

11.play: no need.

12.play: BENVOLIO: Am I like such a fellow?
notes: Am I] CLARKE. The quietness of this retort, with the slight but significant emphasis which we imagine thrown upon the 'I,' admirably gives point to the humorous effect of Mercutio's lecturing Benvolio—the sedate and peace-making Benvolio, and lectured by Mercutio, of all people !—for the sin of quarrelsomeness.

13.play: MERCUTIO: Come, come, thou art as hot a jack in thy

14.play: mood as any in Italy, and as soon moved to be

15.play: moody, and as soon moody to be moved.

16.play: BENVOLIO: And what to?
notes: what to] STA. And what too, of the old copies, means 'And what else?' or 'What more?' [Dyce.

17.play: MERCUTIO: Nay, an there were two such, we should

18.play: have none shortly, for one would kill the other.

19.play: Thou—why, thou wilt quarrel with a man that

20.play: hath a hair more or a hair less in his beard than

21.play: thou hast. Thou wilt quarrel with a man for cracking

22.play: nuts, having no other reason but because thou

23.play: hast hazel eyes. What eye but such an eye would spy

24.play: out such a quarrel? Thy head is as full of quarrels as

25.play: an egg is full of meat, and yet thy head hath been

26.play: beaten as addle as an egg for quarreling. Thou hast

27.play: quarreled with a man for coughing in the street

28.play: because he hath wakened thy dog that hath lain

29.play: asleep in the sun. Didst thou not fall out with a tailor

30.play: for wearing his new doublet before Easter? With

31.play: another, for tying his new shoes with old ribbon?

32.play: And yet thou wilt tutor me from quarreling?
notes: tutor] MAL. Thou wilt endeavor to restrain me, by prudential advice, from quarreling. [Sing.

33.play: BENVOLIO: An I were so apt to quarrel as thou art, any

34.play: man should buy the fee simple of my life for an

35.play: hour and a quarter.

36.play: MERCUTIO: The fee simple? O simple!

37.play: [Enter Tybalt, Petruchio, and others.]

38.play: BENVOLIO: By my head, here comes the Capulets.

39.play: MERCUTIO: By my heel, I care not.

40.play: TYBALT, to his companions

41.play: Follow me close, for I will speak to them.—
notes: Follow me] MAL. I strongly suspect this line and the stage-direction of Q₁ to be an interpolation; for would Tybalt's partisans suffer him to be killed without taking part in the affray? That they do not join in it appears from the account given by Benvolio. [Hal. STEEV. Malone forgets that, even in his own ed., Tybalt is not killed while his partisans are on the stage. They go out with him after he has wounded Mercutio; and he himself re-enters, unattended, when he fights with Romeo. [Hal.

42.play: Gentlemen, good e’en. A word with one of you.
notes: Gentlemen, good den] WALKER ('Vers.,' p. 189). Gentlemen is very often a dissylable. [This line cited.]

43.play: MERCUTIO: And but one word with one of us? Couple it

44.play: with something. Make it a word and a blow.

45.play: TYBALT: You shall find me apt enough to that, sir, an

46.play: you will give me occasion.

47.play: MERCUTIO: Could you not take some occasion without

48.play: giving?

49.play: TYBALT: Mercutio, thou consortest with Romeo.
notes: Consort] SING. To comprehend Mercutio's captious indignation it should be remembered that a consort was the old term for a set or company of musicians, according to Bullokar and Phillips. [Huds. Sta. Dyce. CLARKE. Mercutio, who was an invited guest at Capulet's feast, is so much an intimate of that family that one of its members thinks he has a right to call him to account for his constant association with the son to the head of the rival House.

50.play: MERCUTIO: Consort? What, dost thou make us minstrels?

51.play: An thou make minstrels of us, look to hear

52.play: nothing but discords. Here’s my fiddlestick; here’s

53.play: that shall make you dance. Zounds, consort!
notes: 'Zounds] WHITE. 'Come' of F₁ was in deference to the Stat. 3 Jac. I.

54.play: BENVOLIO: We talk here in the public haunt of men.

55.play: Either withdraw unto some private place,

56.play: Or reason coldly of your grievances,
notes: Or reason] DYCE. A mistake occasioned by the ‘Or’ which commences the next line. WHITE. Benvolio presents a triple alternative: either to withdraw to a private place, or to discuss the matter quietly where they were, or else to part company; and it is supremely in character that on such an occasion he should perceive and suggest all these methods of avoiding public scandal. CLARKE. It is more likely that Benvolio should recommend his friends to retirè and talk over their grievances coolly, than that he should offer them three alternatives.

57.play: Or else depart. Here all eyes gaze on us.
notes: depart] STA. Or else part. See Love’s Lab. L., II, i: ‘Which we much rather had depart withal.’

58.play: MERCUTIO: Men’s eyes were made to look, and let them gaze.

59.play: I will not budge for no man’s pleasure, I.
notes: I will . . . I] STA. The duplication of the pronoun is a construction of frequent use in the language of Sh.’s time. So in The Tempest, III, iii: ‘You are three men of sin, whom destiny (That hath to nstrument this lower world And what is in’t) the never-surfeited sea Hath caus’d to belch up you.’

61.play: TYBALT: Well, peace be with you, sir. Here comes my man.

62.play: MERCUTIO: But I’ll be hanged, sir, if he wear your livery.

63.play: Marry, go before to field, he’ll be your follower.

64.play: Your Worship in that sense may call him “man.”

65.play: TYBALT: Romeo, the love I bear thee can afford
notes: the love] DEL. (‘Lex.’). This is of course ironical. Most edd. adopt the much feebler reading of (Q₁), whereto ‘can afford’ does not exactly apply. An offer or grant of love can be expected, but not of hate. ULR. I follow (Q₁) because Coll.’s (MS.) has ‘hate,’ and because, moreover, Tybalt appears to be too wild and furious to avail himself of ironical expressions.

66.play: No better term than this: thou art a villain.

67.play: ROMEO: Tybalt, the reason that I have to love thee

68.play: Doth much excuse the appertaining rage
notes: excuse] COLL. (ed. 2). The (MS.) means that the love Romeo bears Tybalt goes far beyond the rage he should otherwise have felt at such a greeting.
appertaining rage To] WALKER ('Crit.,' vol. i, p. 162) [cites this as a peculiar construction with the adjective]; that is, rage appertaining to.

69.play: To such a greeting. Villain am I none.

70.play: Therefore farewell. I see thou knowest me not.

71.play: TYBALT: Boy, this shall not excuse the injuries

72.play: That thou hast done me. Therefore turn and draw.

73.play: ROMEO: I do protest I never injured thee
notes: injured] MOMMSEN. The old and especially more correct form, injuried (cf. Nares, ed. Halliw. s. v.), is to be preferred, used as it is here in manifest reference to the preceding substantive, and with a thoroughly denominative application. No one seems to have noticed it; all adopt the more common form, injured, of (Q1) and Q3, or rather the injur’d of F1. In the beginning of the seventeenth century, the old form had already vanished, and was frequently omitted from older pieces by the printers. Yet it is found undoubtedly a few times in Lyly (before 1584), Marlowe (before 1586), and Heywood (before 1604). See Dyce in Marl. I, p. 19. The latter learned commentator, as well as all the others, failed to notice it in this passage, where it is an interesting archaism.

74.play: But love thee better than thou canst devise

75.play: Till thou shalt know the reason of my love.

76.play: And so, good Capulet, which name I tender

77.play: As dearly as mine own, be satisfied.

78.play: MERCUTIO: O calm, dishonorable, vile submission!

79.play: Alla stoccato carries it away.   He draws.
notes: A la stoccata] STEEV. Stoccata is the Italian term for a thrust or stab with a rapier. [Sing. Knt. Verp. Huds. Sta. Ulr. Mercutio uses la stoccata as one word, and places before it the indefinite article a. He intends to say: ‘Only a well-directed thrust carries away this shameful submission.’ Schlegel’s translation is here incorrect. Clarke. Mercutio jocosely gives this term as a title for Tybalt.

80.play: Tybalt, you ratcatcher, will you walk?

81.play: TYBALT: What wouldst thou have with me?

82.play: MERCUTIO: Good king of cats, nothing but one of your
notes: king of cats] MAL. Allud’ng to his name. [Sing. Ulr. Del. Huds.

83.play: nine lives, that I mean to make bold withal, and, as

84.play: you shall use me hereafter, dry-beat the rest of the
notes: dray-beat] CLARKE. That is, severely beat. Dry in the sense of ‘hard,’ ‘severe,’ comes indirectly from ariden, an ancient verb for endure or suffer, and the Scottish and old English verb to ‘dree,’ which has the same meaning. Lord Bacon, and Butler in his ‘Hudibras,’ use the word in this sense. Also it is in Com. of Err. II, ii, 64.

85.play: eight. Will you pluck your sword out of his pilcher
notes: pilcher] WARB. We should read pilche, which signifies a cloak or coat of skins, meaning the scabbard. [Sing. Huds. STEEV. This explanation is, I believe, just. Nash, in Pierce Pennylesse, 1595, speaks of a carman in a leather pilche. [Sing. (ed. 1), Huds. Clarke.] Again, in Decker’s Satiromastix, 1602: ‘I’ll beat five pounds out of his leather pilch.’ Again, ‘Thou hast forgot how thou ambled’st in a leather pilch, by a play-waggon in the highway, and took’st mad Jeronimo’s part, to get service among the mimicks.’ It appears from this passage that Ben Jonson acted the part of Hieronimo in the Spanish tragedy, the speech being addressed to Horace, under which character old Ben is ridiculed. [Ital. NARES. A scabbard [Knt.] from pylche, a skin-coat, Saxon. See Skinner. SING. (ed. 2). There has been a vain attempt to make Pilcher signify a leathern sheath, because a Pilch meant a leathern coat or pelt. It is quite evident that in this jocose, bantering speech Mercutio substitutes Pilcher for Scabbard. The poet was familiar with the proverb ‘Pitchers have ears,’ of which he has twice availed himself. The ears, as every one knows, are the handles, which have since been called the lugs; pitcher was suggested by the play upon the word ears, which is here used for hilts in the plural, according to the usage of the poet’s time. [Sta. STA. A pilch was the name for some outer garment made of leather [‘Pierce Pennylesse,’ 1592, cited], and the word might be applied suitably enough for the leathern sheath of a rapier. Perhaps we should read, ‘out of his pilch, sir,’ &c. COLL. {a2 a2}. No other instance has been adduced of the use of this word in this way in any other author. [Verp. White.] Very likely the last syllable was accidentally added by the printer, and that Mercutio said, ‘Pluck your sword out of his pilch.’ [Ulr. DYCE. A scabbard, a sheath. KTLY. I think the right word is pilche, a leathern coat. In V, i, 202, the sheath of a dagger is termed its house.

86.play: by the ears? Make haste, lest mine be about your
notes: your] DYCE. The ‘the’ [of FF] being evidently an error, for presently after Mercutio twice exclaims, ‘A plague o’ both your houses!’ WHITE. Possibly yr was mistaken for ye, and we should read as afterward, ‘your houses.’

88.play: TYBALT: I am for you.  He draws.

89.play: ROMEO: Gentle Mercutio, put thy rapier up.

90.play: MERCUTIO: Come, sir, your passado.      They fight.

91.play: ROMEO: Draw, Benvolio, beat down their weapons.

93.play: Gentlemen, for shame forbear this outrage!

94.play: Tybalt! Mercutio! The Prince expressly hath

95.play: Forbid this bandying in Verona streets.

97.play: Romeo attempts to beat down their rapiers.

102.play: A plague o’ both houses! I am sped.

103.play: Is he gone and hath nothing?

105.play: MERCUTIO: Ay, ay, a scratch, a scratch. Marry, ’tis enough.

106.play: Where is my page?—Go, villain, fetch a surgeon.

108.play: ROMEO: Courage, man, the hurt cannot be much.

109.play: MERCUTIO: No, ’tis not so deep as a well, nor so wide as

110.play: a church door, but ’tis enough. ’Twill serve. Ask for

111.play: me tomorrow, and you shall find me a grave man. I
notes: grave man] FARMER. This jest was better in old language than it is at present. Lidgate says, in his elegy upon Chaucer: ‘My master Chaucer now is grave.’ [Sing. Hal. STEEV. We meet with the same quibble in The Revenger’s Tragedy, 1608, where Vindice dresses up a lady’s skull and observes: ‘—— she has a somewhat grave look with her.’ [Sta. Hal. MAL. In Sir Thomas Overbury’s Description of a Sexton, Characters, 1616: ‘At every church-style commonly there’s an ale-house; where let him bee found never so idle-pated, hee is still a grave drunkard!’ [Sing. Sta. Hal. COLERIDGE (Lit. Rem. vol. II, p. 156, ed. 1836). How fine an effect the wit and raillery habitual to Mercutio, even struggling with his pain, give to Romeo’s follow- ing speech, and at the same time so completely justifying his passionate revenge on Tybalt! STA. In Italy the funeral follows close upon death; and it was so formerly in England too; hence poor Mercutio’s quibble [Clarke], and the fact of the narcotic administered to Juliet being tempered to operate only ‘two and forty hours,’ are strictly in keeping with the usages of the period. HALLAM (‘Lit. of Europe’). It seems to have been necessary to keep down the other characters that they might not overpower the principal one; and though we can by no means agree with Dryden, that if Sh. had not killed Mercutio, Mercutio would have killed him, there might have been some danger of his killing Romeo. His brilliant vivacity shows the softness of the other a little to a disadvantage. [Verp. VERPLANCK Perhaps Hallam has hit upon the true reason, for it is worthy of note that the death of Mercutio is wholly the Poet’s own invention. It does not come from the poem or novel, where there is merely an accidental contest between the Capulets and Montagues, whom Romeo, endeavoring to part, is assailed by Tybalt, and kills ’im in self-defence, not in anger for the murder of a friend.

112.play: am peppered, I warrant, for this world. A plague o’

113.play: both your houses! Zounds, a dog, a rat, a mouse, a

114.play: cat, to scratch a man to death! A braggart, a rogue, a

115.play: villain that fights by the book of arithmetic! Why the

116.play: devil came you between us? I was hurt under your

118.play: ROMEO: I thought all for the best.

119.play: MERCUTIO: Help me into some house, Benvolio,

120.play: Or I shall faint. A plague o’ both your houses!
notes: your houses] CLARKE. The ineffectual attempt to repeat his former sentence, ‘A plague o’ both your houses!’—the shadowy fragment of the one phrase, ‘your houses!’ being but an insubstantial representation of the other—serves exquisitely to indicate the faint speech of the dying man, and poetically to image his failing powers.

121.play: They have made worms’ meat of me.

122.play: I have it, and soundly, too. Your houses!

124.play: ROMEO: This gentleman, the Prince’s near ally,

125.play: My very friend, hath got this mortal hurt
notes: got his] MOMMSEN. The universally adopted reading, got his, dates merely from a misprint in Q3, gott his, from which Q4 and F1 made got his.

126.play: In my behalf. My reputation stained

127.play: With Tybalt’s slander—Tybalt, that an hour

128.play: Hath been my cousin! O sweet Juliet,

129.play: Thy beauty hath made me effeminate

130.play: And in my temper softened valor’s steel.

132.play: BENVOLIO: O Romeo, Romeo, brave Mercutio is dead.

133.play: That gallant spirit hath aspired the clouds,
notes: aspired] STeev. In Greene's Card of Fancy, 1608: 'Her haughty mind is too lofty for me to aspire.' In Chapman's Ninth Iliad: '—— and aspir'd the gods' eternal seats.' We never use this verb at present without some particle, as to and after. [Sing. Sta. MAL. Also Marlowe, in his Tamburlaine, 1590: 'And both our souls aspire celestial thrones.' [Sing. Sta. STA. So to the word arrive we always add at, unto, or in; but the old writers frequently adopted the construction in the text. And our author, 3 Hen. VI. V, iii, 8: '—— those powers that the Queen Hath raised in Gallia have arriv'd the coast.' WHITE. As we now use attain.

134.play: Which too untimely here did scorn the earth.

135.play: ROMEO: This day’s black fate on more days doth depend.
notes: This day's, &c.] JOHNS. This day's unhappy destiny hangs over the days yet to come. There will yet be more mischief. [Sing. Huds.

136.play: This but begins the woe others must end.

138.play: BENVOLIO: Here comes the furious Tybalt back again.

139.play: ROMEO: Alive in triumph, and Mercutio slain!
notes: triumph!] ULR. It seems to me 'He gone' accords much better with the following 'in triumph.'

respective lenity] MAL. Cool, considerate gentleness. [Sing. Huds. Dyce. S. WALKER, (‘Crit.,’ vol. i, p. 180), cites this as an exception under his ‘Art. xxviii. Perspective, directive, &c., are frequently used by Sh. and his contemporaries, so to speak, in a passive sense.’

141.play: And fire-eyed fury be my conduct now.—
notes: conduct] MAL. It here means imagination. [Sing.] So in The Rape of Lucrece: '—— which the conceited painter drew,' &c. [Sta. CRAIK ['Eirg. of Sh.,' p. 135]. To conceit is another form of our still familiar to conceive. And the noun conceit, which survives with a limited meaning (the conception of a man by himself, which is so apt to be one of over-estimation), is also frequent in Sh. with the sense, nearly, of what we now call conception, in general. Sometimes it is used in a sense which might almost be said to be the opposite of what it now means; as when Juliet [in this passage] employs it as the term to denote her all-absorbing affection for Romeo; or as when Gratiano, in the Mer. of Ven., I, i, 90, speaks of a sort of men, who 'do a wilful stillness entertain, With purpose to be dress'd in an opinion Of wisdom, gravity, profound conceit'— that is, deep thought. So, again, when Rosaline, in Love's Lah., II, i, 72, speaking of Biron, describes his 'fair tongue' as 'conceit's expositor,' all that she means is, that speech is the expounder of thought. The scriptural expression, still in familiar use, 'wise in his own conceit,' means merely wise in his own thought, or in his own eyes, as we are told in the margin the Hebrew literally signifies. In the New Testament, where we have 'in their own conceits,' the Greek is simply παρ' ἑαυτοῖς (in or with themselves).

142.play: Now, Tybalt, take the “villain” back again

143.play: That late thou gavest me, for Mercutio’s soul

144.play: Is but a little way above our heads,

145.play: Staying for thine to keep him company.

146.play: Either thou or I, or both, must go with him.

147.play: TYBALT: Thou wretched boy that didst consort him here

152.play: The citizens are up, and Tybalt slain.

153.play: Stand not amazed. The Prince will doom thee death

154.play: If thou art taken. Hence, be gone, away.

155.play: ROMEO: O, I am Fortune’s fool!
notes: fortune’s fool] JOHNS. I am always running in the way of evil fortune like the Fool in the play. ‘Thou art death’s fool,’ in Meas. for Meas. [Sing. Hal. DOUCE. There is certainly no allusion to any play. Sh. is very fond of alluding to the mockery of fortune. Thus we have, ‘Ye fools of fortune.’—Tim. of Athens. ‘I am the natural fool of fortune.’—Lear. In the last passage a pointed allusion is made to the idiot fool. Sir J. Suckling uses the same expression in his play of The Goblins; and Hamlet speaks of ‘the fools of nature,’ precisely in the same sense. [Sing. Hal. SING. In Julius Cæsar the expression is, ‘He is but fortune’s knave.’ [Hal. STA. I am the sport of fortune. CLARKE. It has reference to the ‘fool’ in the old mysteries, moralities, or dramatic shows, who is represented as the perpetual object of pursuit, mockery, and disaster.

159.play: CITIZEN: Which way ran he that killed Mercutio?

160.play: Tybalt, that murderer, which way ran he?

162.play: CITIZEN, to Tybalt  Up, sir, go with me.

163.play: I charge thee in the Prince’s name, obey.

164.play: [Enter Prince, old Montague, Capulet, their Wives and all.]

165.play: PRINCE: Where are the vile beginners of this fray?

166.play: BENVOLIO: O noble prince, I can discover all

167.play: The unlucky manage of this fatal brawl.

168.play: There lies the man, slain by young Romeo,

169.play: That slew thy kinsman, brave Mercutio.

170.play: LADY CAPULET: Tybalt, my cousin, O my brother’s child!

171.play: O prince! O cousin! Husband! O, the blood is spilled
notes: O prince! &c.] KNT. (ed. 2). Some modern eds. in this and in other passages have adopted the arbitrary course of making up a text out of (Q1) and Q2 without regard to the important circumstance that this later edition was ‘newly corrected, augmented, and amended,’—and that the folio, in nearly every essential particular, follows it.

172.play: Of my dear kinsman! Prince, as thou art true,
notes: as thou art true] JOHNS. As thou art just and upright. [Sing. STEEV. In Rich. III: I, i, 36, ‘And if King Edward be as true and just.’ [Sing.

173.play: For blood of ours, shed blood of Montague.

notes: O cousin!] DYCE. This line is no doubt corrupted; ‘cozin’ would seem to have crept into it, in consequence of the transcriber’s or printer’s eye having caught that word just above. [White.

175.play: PRINCE: Benvolio, who began this bloody fray?

176.play: BENVOLIO: Tybalt, here slain, whom Romeo’s hand did slay—
notes: Tybalt here slain, &c.] Bos. In this speech of Benvolio’s, as given in (Q1), the reader will find, I apprehend, both in the rhythm and construction, a much greater resemblance to the style of some of Sh.’s predecessors than to his own. [White. WHITE. [‘Introd.,’ p. 27]. But if the reader will compare this speech with that of Q2, I think that he will agree with me that it is but another of those passages already alluded to, in which an inferior writer attempted to supply deficiencies in the report of the genuine speech. At least, it is not the work of any 'predecessor' of Sh. WHITE. [‘Introd.,’ p. 27]. But if the reader will compare this speech with that

177.play: Romeo, that spoke him fair, bid him bethink

178.play: How nice the quarrel was, and urged withal
notes: nice] JOHNS. How slight, how unimportant, how petty. So in V, ii, 18. [Sing. Coll. Sta. SING. (ed. 1). It here means silly, trifling, or wanton. [In note on Tam. of Shr., III, i, 80.] Chaucer's use of Nice seems to point at the old Fr. Nice, Niais, silly, weak, simple, which sense suits the passages in Rom. and Jul. [Substantially, Knt. Coll. Huds. STA. It here signifies not delicate, squeamish, &c., as in some other instances in these plays, but trivial, unimportant.

179.play: Your high displeasure. All this utterèd

180.play: With gentle breath, calm look, knees humbly bowed

181.play: Could not take truce with the unruly spleen

182.play: Of Tybalt, deaf to peace, but that he tilts

183.play: With piercing steel at bold Mercutio’s breast,
notes: Mercutio's breast] COLERIDGE (‘Lit. Rem.’ vol. ii, p. 156). This small portion of untruth in Benvolio's narrative is finely conceived. [Huds.

184.play: Who, all as hot, turns deadly point to point

185.play: And, with a martial scorn, with one hand beats

186.play: Cold death aside and with the other sends

187.play: It back to Tybalt, whose dexterity

188.play: Retorts it. Romeo he cries aloud
notes: Retorts it] SING. (‘Sh. Vindicated,’ p. 232). The interpolation of the word home [by Coll. (MS.)] is an unlicensed liberty, and would require better authority than that of the (MS.) to induce us to admit it into the text.

189.play: “Hold, friends! Friends, part!” and swifter than his

191.play: His agile arm beats down their fatal points,

192.play: And ’twixt them rushes; underneath whose arm

193.play: An envious thrust from Tybalt hit the life

194.play: Of stout Mercutio, and then Tybalt fled.

195.play: But by and by comes back to Romeo,

196.play: Who had but newly entertained revenge,

197.play: And to ’t they go like lightning, for ere I

198.play: Could draw to part them was stout Tybalt slain,

199.play: And, as he fell, did Romeo turn and fly.

200.play: This is the truth, or let Benvolio die.

201.play: LADY CAPULET: He is a kinsman to the Montague.

202.play: Affection makes him false; he speaks not true.
notes: false] JOHNSON. The charge of falsehood on Benvolio, though produced at hazard, is very just. The author, who seems to intend the character of Benvolio as good, meant, perhaps, to show how the best minds, in a state of faction and discord, are detorted to criminal partiality. [Sing. Knt. Corn. Verp. Hal. KNT. There is a slight particle of untruth in Benvolio's statement, which, to a certain degree, justifies this charge of Lady Capulet. Tybalt was bent on quarrelling with Romeo, but Mercutio forced on his own quarrel with Tybalt. Dr. Johnson's remark upon this circumstance is worthy of his character as a moralist.

203.play: Some twenty of them fought in this black strife,

204.play: And all those twenty could but kill one life.

205.play: I beg for justice, which thou, prince, must give.

206.play: Romeo slew Tybalt; Romeo must not live.

207.play: PRINCE: Romeo slew him; he slew Mercutio.

208.play: Who now the price of his dear blood doth owe?

209.play: MONTAGUE: Not Romeo, Prince; he was Mercutio’s friend.

210.play: His fault concludes but what the law should end,

213.play: Immediately we do exile him hence.

214.play: I have an interest in your hearts’ proceeding:

215.play: My blood for your rude brawls doth lie a-bleeding.
notes: My blood] S. WALKER. That is, my kinsman; sanguis meus.

216.play: But I’ll amerce you with so strong a fine

217.play: That you shall all repent the loss of mine.

218.play: I will be deaf to pleading and excuses.

219.play: Nor tears nor prayers shall purchase out abuses.

220.play: Therefore use none. Let Romeo hence in haste,
notes: I will] MOMM. Q₂ has It, referring to blood,—compare Gen. iv. 10, the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto Me from the ground,—to me this interpretation is very beautiful.

221.play: Else, when he is found, that hour is his last.

222.play: Bear hence this body and attend our will.

223.play: Mercy but murders, pardoning those that kill.
notes: Mercy but murders] MAL. So in Hale’s Memorials: ‘When I find myself swayed to mercy, let me remember likewise that there is a mercy due to the country.’ [Sing. Hal. MAL. So in Stubbes’s Anatomie of Abuses, 2d part: ‘And yet let the Prince be sure of this, to answere at the day of judgment before the tribunall seate of God for all the offences that the partie pardoned shall commit at any time of his life after. For if the Prince had cutte him off when the lawe had passed on him, that evill had not been committed. To this purpose I remember I have heard a certeine pretie apotheque [apothegme] uttered by a jester to a king. The king had pardoned one of his subjects that had committed murther, who, being pardoned, committed the like offence againe, and by meanes was pardoned the second time also, and yet filling up the measure of his iniquitie, killed the third, and being brought before the king, the king being verie sorie, asked him why he had killed three men, to whom his jester, standing by, replied, saieing, No (O King) he killed but the first, and thou hast killed the other two; for if thou hadst hanged him up at the first, the other two nad not beene killed; therefore thou hast killed them, and shall answere for their bloud. Which thing being heard, the king hanged him up straightway, as he very well deserved.’ [Hal. COLL. (ed. 2). In F1 is another of the places in which the old printers confounded “but” and not.

2.play: JULIET: Gallop apace, you fiery-footed steeds,
notes: Gallop apace] MAL. Sh. probably remembered Marlowe’s King Edward II, which was performed before 1593: ‘Gallop apace, bright Phœbus, through the skie, And dusky night in rusty iron car; Between you both, shorten the time, I pray, That I may see that most desired day.’ So in Barnabe Riche’s Farewell: ‘The day to his seeming passed away so slowely that he had thought the stately steedes had bin tired that drawe the chariot of the Sunne, and wished that Phaeton had beene there with a whippe.’ The first ed. of Riche’s Farewell was printed in 1583. [Sing.

3.play: Towards Phoebus’ lodging. Such a wagoner
notes: lodging] ULR. A majority of the edd. prefer ‘mansion.’ I see not why. DEL. Because it sounds more stately. DYCE. Lodging seems preferable, to say nothing of the word ‘mansions’ occurring towards the end of this speech. (Compare Petrarch, Canzone v: ‘Quando vede ’l pastor calare i raggi Del gran pianeta al nido ov’ egli alberga,’ &c.) WHITE. ‘Mansion’ is more ambitious, but less appropriate.

4.play: As Phaëton would whip you to the west

5.play: And bring in cloudy night immediately.

6.play: Spread thy close curtain, love-performing night,

7.play: That runaways’ eyes may wink, and Romeo
notes: runaways] The notes upon this word will be found in the Appen<jix.

8.play: Leap to these arms, untalked of and unseen.

9.play: Lovers can see to do their amorous rites

10.play: By their own beauties, or, if love be blind,
notes: their own beauties] MAL. So in Marlowe’s Hero and Leander: ‘—— dark night is Cupid’s day.’ [Sing. STEEV. Milton, in his Comus, might here have been indebted to Sh.: ‘Virtue could see to do what virtue would, By her own radiant light, though sun and moon Were in the flat sea sunk.’ [Sing. Sta.

11.play: It best agrees with night. Come, civil night,
notes: civil] JOHNSON. That is, grave, decently solemn. [Sing. Huds.

12.play: Thou sober-suited matron all in black,

13.play: And learn me how to lose a winning match

14.play: Played for a pair of stainless maidenhoods.

15.play: Hood my unmanned blood, bating in my cheeks,
notes: Hood . . . . bating] STEEV. These are terms of falconry. An unmanned hawk is one that is not brought to endure company. Bating is fluttering with the wings as striving to fly away. [Sing. Coll. Verp. Huds. White, Cham. KNT. To man a hawk was to accustom her to the falconer who trained her. STA. The hood was the cap with which the hawk was usually hoodwinked. An unmanned hawk was one not sufficiently trained to be familiar with her keeper, and such birds commonly fluttered and beat their wings violently in efforts to escape. See also Tam. of Shr., IV, i, 206. DYCE. The hawk was hooded till let fly at the game. NARES. To bate, probably from battre, Fr. It is a natural action with birds, after bathing, to shake the moisture from their wings; also when desirous of their food, or prey. The true meaning of the word is beautifully exemplified in the following passage from Bacon: 'Wherein (viz., in matters of business) I would to God that I were hooded, that I saw less; or that I could perform more, for now I am like a hawk that bates, when I see occasion of service; but cannot fly because I am ty'd to another's fist.' DYCE. 'Bate, Bateing or Bateth, is when the Hawk fluttereth with her Wings either from Pearch or Fist, as it were striving to get away; also it is taken for her striving with her Prey, and not forsaking it till it be overcome.'—R. Holme's Academy of Armory and Blazon, B. ii, c. xi, p. 238.

grown] COLL. (ed. 1). Rowe's change was scarcely necessary. KEIGHTLEY. Rowe was probably right. Still, when we consider the joyous perturbation of Juliet's mind, there may be an asyndeton, and she may be speaking allo staccato.

16.play: With thy black mantle till strange love grow bold,
notes: strange] CLARKE. That is, reserved, retiring.

17.play: Think true love acted simple modesty.

18.play: Come, night. Come, Romeo. Come, thou day in

19.play: night,

20.play: For thou wilt lie upon the wings of night

21.play: Whiter than new snow upon a raven’s back.

22.play: Come, gentle night; come, loving black-browed
notes: black-browed] STEEV. In King John, V, vi, 17: 'Why, here walk I, in the black brow of night.' [Sing.

23.play: night,

24.play: Give me my Romeo, and when I shall die,
notes: he shall] DEL. Juliet demands for herself the life-long possession of her lover, and not until after her death may Night, as her heiress, carry away Romeo. Of the possibility of Romeo's death she cannot, in her present happiness, conceive. [Uhr.

25.play: Take him and cut him out in little stars,

26.play: And he will make the face of heaven so fine

27.play: That all the world will be in love with night

28.play: And pay no worship to the garish sun.
notes: garish] JOHNSON. Milton had this speech in his thoughts when he wrote the following lines in 'II Penseroso:' 'Till civil-suited morn appear,' and 'Hide me from day's garish eye.' [Sing. Sta. STEEV. Garish is gaudy, showy. [Sing. Huds.] In Rich. III: IV, iv, 89. In Marlowe's Edward II, 1598: '---- march'd like players With garish robes.' It sometimes signifies wild, flighty. Thus, '---- starting up and garishly staring about, especially on the face of Eliosto.'— 'Hinde's Eliosto Libidinoso, 1606. [Hal liwell, To Triumph, 1586, speaks of 'garish ladies;' and Shirley (The Triumph of Peace, 1633) has 'garish habits.' See also Cymbeline, III, iv, 87. Dyce, following Johnson, says 'garish' is here equivalent to 'fierce;' but the word is never used in that sense.

29.play: O, I have bought the mansion of a love

30.play: But not possessed it, and, though I am sold,

31.play: Not yet enjoyed. So tedious is this day

32.play: As is the night before some festival

33.play: To an impatient child that hath new robes

34.play: And may not wear them.

35.play: [Enter Nurse with cords.]

36.play: O, here comes my nurse,

37.play: And she brings news, and every tongue that speaks

38.play: But Romeo’s name speaks heavenly eloquence.—

39.play: Now, nurse, what news? What hast thou there? The

40.play: cords

41.play: That Romeo bid thee fetch?

42.play: NURSE: Ay, ay, the cords.

43.play: Dropping the rope ladder.

44.play: JULIET: Ay me, what news? Why dost thou wring thy hands?

45.play: NURSE: Ah weraday, he’s dead, he’s dead, he’s dead!

46.play: We are undone, lady, we are undone.
notes: We are, &c.] KTYL. ('Milton,' vol. i, p. 110). This line consists of two choriambs, with an intermediate trochee; and there must be a pause at the end of each. So Milton (Comus, v. 666): 'Why are you vext, Lady? why do you frown?'

47.play: Alack the day, he’s gone, he’s killed, he’s dead.

48.play: JULIET: Can heaven be so envious?
notes: envious.] WHITE. So malicious.

49.play: NURSE: Romeo can,

50.play: Though heaven cannot. O Romeo, Romeo,
notes: Romeo !] DEL. I doubt that this is here to be considered an exclamation; but it is rather the beginning of a sentence which the Nurse's grief will not permit her to finish.

51.play: Whoever would have thought it? Romeo!

52.play: JULIET: What devil art thou that dost torment me thus?

53.play: This torture should be roared in dismal hell.

54.play: Hath Romeo slain himself? Say thou but “Ay,”

55.play: And that bare vowel “I” shall poison more
notes: but ‘I, I’] THEOB. At Sh.'s time of day the affirmative adverb Ay was generally written I: and by this means it both becomes a vowel, and answers in sound to Eye, upon which the conceit turns in the second line. [Substantially, Mal. Sing. Knlt. Corn. Verp. Coll. Huds. Sta. White, Dyce. CORN. The edd. have here thought it necessary to retain the old spelling [I for ay]. We have, however, ventured to deviate from this unsightly practice, conceiving that there is sufficient similarity between the sounds of ‘ay’ and ‘I’ to point out the intended quibble. This is one of the trivial passages which we easily persuade ourselves have, by some accident or impertinence, been foisted into the genuine text. COLL. (ed. 2). ‘That bare vowel’ it is obviously necessary to retain here; but elsewhere we adopt the modern form. WHITE. It has been necessary to retain the simple vowel I twice in this passage.

56.play: Than the death-darting eye of cockatrice.
notes: cockatrice] REED [Note on 2 Hen. VI : III, ii, 52]. In Albion’s England, b. i, c. iii: ‘— As Æsculap an herdsman did espie, That did with easy sight enforce a basilisk to flye, Albeit naturally that beast doth murther with the eye.’ [Sing. Huds.] NARES. An imaginary creature, supposed to be produced from a cock’s egg; a production long thought to be real. It was said to be in form like a serpent, with the head of a cock. Sir Thomas Browne, however, distinguishes it from the ancient basilisk; and in so doing describes it more particularly. Vide Eng. into Vulg.

57.play: I am not I if there be such an “I,”

58.play: Or those eyes shut that makes thee answer “Ay.”

59.play: If he be slain, say “Ay,” or if not, “No.”

60.play: Brief sounds determine my weal or woe.

61.play: NURSE: I saw the wound. I saw it with mine eyes

62.play: (God save the mark!) here on his manly breast—

63.play: A piteous corse, a bloody piteous corse,

64.play: Pale, pale as ashes, all bedaubed in blood,

65.play: All in gore blood. I swoonèd at the sight.
notes: gore blood] FORBY. That is, clotted, congealed blood. The words separately used are doubtless general, but thus combined seem to be provincial. Certainly archaic. As the Nurse says of Tybalt, ‘all in gore-blood,’ exactly so would an East-Anglian nurse say on a like occasion. Or, perhaps ‘all of a gore,’ or ‘all of a gore of blood.’ [Ulal.

swounded] WHITE. Proper as ‘swouned’ may be under other circumstances, is there not something gained by leaving the vulgar form of the word in the Nurse’s mouth? DYCE (ed. 2). [Note on Wint. Tale, IV, iii, 13]. Malone says ‘swoon, in the old copies of these plays, is ALWAYS written sound or swound.’ Yet Malone might have found in F1, ‘Many will swoon when they do,’ &c., As You Like It, IV, iii, 159. ‘Or else I swoone with this death-killing,’ &c., Rich. III: IV, i, 35. ‘What? doth shee SWONNE,’ 3 Hen. VI: V, v, 45.

66.play: JULIET: O break, my heart, poor bankrout, break at once!
notes: bankrupt] KNT. We restore the old poetical bankrout in preference to the modern bankrupt.

67.play: To prison, eyes; ne’er look on liberty.

68.play: Vile earth to earth resign; end motion here,

69.play: And thou and Romeo press one heavy bier.

70.play: NURSE: O Tybalt, Tybalt, the best friend I had!

72.play: That ever I should live to see thee dead!

73.play: JULIET: What storm is this that blows so contrary?

74.play: Is Romeo slaughtered and is Tybalt dead?

75.play: My dearest cousin, and my dearer lord?
notes: dear-loved] DEL. The QqFF contain a more pregnant construction than that of (Q1), since the comparative dearer transcends the superlative dearest. [ULR. The comparative dearer gives the highest expression to the highest height of Love (die höchste Höhe der Liebe höchst ausdrucksvoll bezeichnet.).

76.play: Then, dreadful trumpet, sound the general doom,

77.play: For who is living if those two are gone?

78.play: NURSE: Tybalt is gone and Romeo banishèd.
notes: Romeo banished] HERAUD (‘Sh.’s Inner Life,’ 1865, p. 61). It must have struck every reader that both Romeo and Juliet’s excessive lamentations for his banishment from Verona rather want motive. Why could not Juliet have gone with him? and, by so doing, have prevented the after evils, which originate solely in their apparently needless separation. Brooke’s poem supplies the hiatus. Juliet there supplicates her lover for his permission to be his companion in exile. But he gives her the reasons why this cannot be: ‘For, but thou change thy mynde, (I do foretell the end) Thou shalt undoo thyselfe for aye, and me thy trusty frende. For why, thy absence knowne, thy father will be wroth, And in his rage no [so] narowly he will pursue us both, That we shall trye in vayne to scape away by flight, And vainely seeke a looking place to hide us from his sight. Then we, found out and caught, quite voydc of strong defence, Shall cruelly be punished for thy departure hence; I as a ravishor, thou as a careles childe, I as a man who doth defile, thou as a mayde defilde.’

79.play: Romeo that killed him—he is banishèd.

80.play: JULIET: O God, did Romeo’s hand shed Tybalt’s blood?

81.play: NURSE: It did, it did, alas the day, it did.

82.play: JULIET: O serpent heart hid with a flow’ring face!
notes: O serpent, &c.] HENLEY. So in Macbeth, I, v, 66. [Sing. MAL. So in King John, II, i, 68: ‘With ladies faces, and fierce dragons spleens.’ Again in Hen. VIII: III, i, 145: ‘You have angel’s faces, but Heaven knows your hearts.’ [Sing.

83.play: Did ever dragon keep so fair a cave?

87.play: Just opposite to what thou justly seem’st,

88.play: A damnèd saint, an honorable villain.

89.play: O nature, what hadst thou to do in hell

90.play: When thou didst bower the spirit of a fiend
notes: bower] COLL. (ed. 2). We hesitate to alter here, because ‘bower’ is very intelligible and figuratively beautiful in connection with ‘paradise;’ but the (MS.) has, rather prosaically, pour, which, however, was formerly often spelt power.

91.play: In mortal paradise of such sweet flesh?

92.play: Was ever book containing such vile matter

93.play: So fairly bound? O, that deceit should dwell

96.play: No faith, no honesty in men. All perjured,

97.play: All forsworn, all naught, all dissemblers.

98.play: Ah, where’s my man? Give me some aqua vitae.

99.play: These griefs, these woes, these sorrows make me

notes: For such a wish] COLERIDGE (‘Lit. Rem.,’ vol. ii, p. 156). Note the Nurse’s mistake of the mind’s audible struggle with itself for its decisions in toto. [Huds.

103.play: For such a wish! He was not born to shame.

104.play: Upon his brow shame is ashamed to sit,
notes: to sit] STEEV. So in Painter’s Palace of Pleasure, tom. ii, p. 223: ‘Is it possible that under such beautie and rare comelinesse, disloyalite and treason may have their siedge and lodging?’ [Sing.

105.play: For ’tis a throne where honor may be crowned

106.play: Sole monarch of the universal Earth.

107.play: O, what a beast was I to chide at him!

108.play: NURSE: Will you speak well of him that killed your cousin?

109.play: JULIET: Shall I speak ill of him that is my husband?

110.play: Ah, poor my lord, what tongue shall smooth thy
notes: smooth] STEEV. This means, in ancient language, to stroke, to caress, to fondle. [Dyce. SING. To smooth is to flatter, to speak fair. It is here metaphorically used to mitigate or assuage the asperity of censure with which Romeo’s name would now be mentioned. [Huds.

112.play: When I, thy three-hours wife, have mangled it?

113.play: But wherefore, villain, didst thou kill my cousin?

114.play: That villain cousin would have killed my husband.

115.play: Back, foolish tears, back to your native spring;
notes: foolish tears] STEEV. So in The Tempest, III, i, 73: ‘—— I am a fool To weep at what I am glad of.’ [Sing.

116.play: Your tributary drops belong to woe,

117.play: Which you, mistaking, offer up to joy.
notes: offer up to joy] ULR. The metaphor here is somewhat forced, and recalls the mode of expression of the Italian Poets.

118.play: My husband lives, that Tybalt would have slain,

119.play: And Tybalt’s dead, that would have slain my

121.play: All this is comfort. Wherefore weep I then?

122.play: Some word there was, worser than Tybalt’s death,

123.play: That murdered me. I would forget it fain,

124.play: But, O, it presses to my memory

125.play: Like damnèd guilty deeds to sinners’ minds:

128.play: Hath slain ten thousand Tybalts. Tybalt’s death
notes: Hath slain ten thousand] M. MASON. That is, worse than the loss of ten thousand Tybalts. [Sing. Huds.]

129.play: Was woe enough if it had ended there;

130.play: Or, if sour woe delights in fellowship
notes: sour woe, &c.] STEEV. Thus the Latin hexameter (I know not whence it comes): ‘Solamen miseris socios habuisse doloris.’ [Sta. ULR. This corresponds to our proverbial phrase, ‘Misfortune never comes alone.’ Steevens and Malone erroneously take the words in the sense of the familiar Latin verse: Solamen, &c.

131.play: And needly will be ranked with other griefs,
notes: needly] CLARKE. Sh. has here coined an excellent word, which is not given among dictionary words, but which it would be well to adopt into our language as good English.

132.play: Why followed not, when she said “Tybalt’s dead,”

133.play: “Thy father” or “thy mother,” nay, or both,

134.play: Which modern lamentation might have moved?
notes: modern] STEEV. It means trite, common. So in As You Like It, II, vii, 156: ‘Full of wise saws and modern instances.’ [Sing. Huds. Sta. STA. That is, ordinary, well-known lamentation. So in All’s Well, II, iii, 2. DYCE. ‘Per modo tutto fuor del modern’ uso.’—Dante, Purg. xvi, 42; where Biagioli remarks: ‘Moderno, s’ usa qui in senso di ordinario.’

135.play: But with a rearward following Tybalt’s death,
notes: rear-ward] COLL. (ed. 2). Might we not read rear-word, though the old copies are uniform. DYCE (ed. 2). ‘Perhaps Collier’s conjecture is right.’—W. N. LETTSOM.

136.play: “Romeo is banishèd.” To speak that word

137.play: Is father, mother, Tybalt, Romeo, Juliet,

138.play: All slain, all dead. “Romeo is banishèd.”

139.play: There is no end, no limit, measure, bound,

140.play: In that word’s death. No words can that woe sound.

141.play: Where is my father and my mother, nurse?

142.play: NURSE: Weeping and wailing over Tybalt’s corse.

143.play: Will you go to them? I will bring you thither.

144.play: JULIET: Wash they his wounds with tears? Mine shall be
notes: with tears:] STA. All the modern eds. place a note of interrogation after these words, but perhaps in error. The Nurse tells Juliet her father and mother are weeping over Tybalt's corse, and asks if she will go to them; to which Juliet replies, 'No, let them wash his wounds with tears; mine shall be spent in wailing Romeo's banishment.' S. WALKER. Point with the folio—'Wash—tears: mine,' &c., abluant. [The words “Wash—tears: mine,” &c., abluant. [The note of interrogation was introduced by Pope. Dyce and Staunton have recently restored the punctuation of the old copies.—Foot-note by LETTSOM.]

146.play: When theirs are dry, for Romeo’s banishment.—

148.play: The Nurse picks up the rope ladder.

150.play: Both you and I, for Romeo is exiled.

151.play: He made you for a highway to my bed,

152.play: But I, a maid, die maiden-widowèd.

153.play: Come, cords—come, nurse. I’ll to my wedding bed,

154.play: And death, not Romeo, take my maidenhead!

155.play: NURSE: Hie to your chamber. I’ll find Romeo

156.play: To comfort you. I wot well where he is.

157.play: Hark you, your Romeo will be here at night.

158.play: I’ll to him. He is hid at Lawrence’ cell.

159.play: JULIET: O, find him!    Giving the Nurse a ring.

160.play: Give this ring to my true knight

161.play: And bid him come to take his last farewell.

2.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Romeo, come forth; come forth, thou fearful man.

3.play: Affliction is enamored of thy parts,

4.play: And thou art wedded to calamity.

5.play: [Enter Romeo.]

6.play: ROMEO: Father, what news? What is the Prince’s doom?

7.play: What sorrow craves acquaintance at my hand

8.play: That I yet know not?

9.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Too familiar

10.play: Is my dear son with such sour company.

11.play: I bring thee tidings of the Prince’s doom.

12.play: ROMEO: What less than doomsday is the Prince’s doom?

13.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: A gentler judgment vanished from his lips:
notes: vanish’d] KTLY. I have never met with any sense of ‘vanish’ but its ordinary one, which certainly will not suit here. We should therefore, I think, read issued or some word of similar meaning. It is curious that Massinger seems to have taken ‘vanish’d’ on Sh.’s authority. ‘Upon those lips from which those sweet words vanish’d.’—Reneg., v. 5. We have, however, in Lucrece: ‘To make more vent for passage of her breath, Which, thronging through her lips, so vanisheth As smoke from Ætna, that in air consumes.’ But the breath is material.

14.play: Not body’s death, but body’s banishment.

15.play: ROMEO: Ha, banishment? Be merciful, say “death,”

16.play: For exile hath more terror in his look,

17.play: Much more than death. Do not say “banishment.”

18.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Here from Verona art thou banishèd.

19.play: Be patient, for the world is broad and wide.

20.play: ROMEO: There is no world without Verona walls

21.play: But purgatory, torture, hell itself.

22.play: Hence “banishèd” is “banished from the world,”

23.play: And world’s exile is death. Then “banishèd”

24.play: Is death mistermed. Calling death “banishèd,”
notes: ‘banished’] HERAUD (‘Sh.’s Inner Life,’ 1865, p. 61). It must have struck every reader that both Romeo and Juliet’s excessive lamentations for his banishment from Verona rather want motive. Why could not Juliet have gone with him? and, by so doing, have prevented the after evils, which originate solely in their apparently needless separation. Brooke’s poem supplies the hiatus. Juliet there supplicates her lover for his permission to be his companion in exile. But he gives her the reasons why this cannot be: ‘For, but thou change thy mynde, (I do foretell the end) Thou shalt undoo thyselfe for aye, and me thy trusty frende. For why, thy absence knowne, thy father will be wroth, And in his rage no [so] narowly he will pursue us both, That we shall trye in vayne to scape away by flight, And vainely seeke a looking place to hide us from his sight. Then we, found out and caught, quite voyde of strong defence, Shall cruelly be punished for thy departure hence; I as a ravishor, thou as a careles childe, I as a man who doth defile, thou as a mayde defilde.’ These reasons Sh. left to the imagination of his audience, or perhaps to their memory.

25.play: Thou cutt’st my head off with a golden ax

26.play: And smilest upon the stroke that murders me.

27.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: O deadly sin, O rude unthankfulness!

28.play: Thy fault our law calls death, but the kind prince,

29.play: Taking thy part, hath rushed aside the law
notes: rush’d] KTYL. Would not push’d be better? As in Hen. V: 1, i, 5: But that the scambling and unquiet time Did push it out of further question.’

30.play: And turned that black word “death” to

31.play: “banishment.”

32.play: This is dear mercy, and thou seest it not.
notes: dear mercy] STEEV. (Q1) reads ‘mere mercy,’ i. e., absolute mercy. [Sing.

33.play: ROMEO: ’Tis torture and not mercy. Heaven is here
notes: heaven is here] STEEV. From this, and the foregoing speech of Romeo, Dryden has borrowed, in his beautiful paraphrase of Chaucer’s Palamon and Arcite: ‘Heaven is not, but where Emily abides And where she’s absent, all is hell besides.’ [Sing. COLERIDGE (‘Lit. Rem.’ vol. ii, p. 157). All deep passions are a sort of atheists, that believe no future.

34.play: Where Juliet lives, and every cat and dog

35.play: And little mouse, every unworthy thing,

36.play: Live here in heaven and may look on her,

37.play: But Romeo may not. More validity,
notes: validity] STEEV. This is employed to signify worth or value in Lear I, i, 83. [Sing.

38.play: More honorable state, more courtship lives
notes: courtship] SING. (ed. 1). By courtship, courtesy, courtly behavior is meant. Bullokar defines ‘compliment to be ceremony, court-ship, fine behavior.’ See also Cotgrave in Curtisanie and Curialité; and Florio in Cortegiana. ‘Would I might never excel a Dutch skipper in courtship, if I did not put distaste into my carriage of purpose.’—Sir Giles Goosecap. Again, in the same play: ‘My lord, my want of courtship makes me fear I should be rude.’

39.play: In carrion flies than Romeo. They may seize

40.play: On the white wonder of dear Juliet’s hand

41.play: And steal immortal blessing from her lips,

42.play: Who even in pure and vestal modesty

43.play: Still blush, as thinking their own kisses sin;

44.play: But Romeo may not; he is banishèd.

45.play: Flies may do this, but I from this must fly.

46.play: They are free men, but I am banishèd.

47.play: And sayest thou yet that exile is not death?

48.play: Hadst thou no poison mixed, no sharp-ground

49.play: knife,

50.play: No sudden mean of death, though ne’er so mean,

52.play: O friar, the damnèd use that word in hell.

53.play: Howling attends it. How hast thou the heart,

54.play: Being a divine, a ghostly confessor,

55.play: A sin absolver, and my friend professed,

56.play: To mangle me with that word “banishèd”?
notes: word] WHITE. The reading of (Q1) has been hitherto retained, although the change in Q2 seems plainly to have been made to avoid the unpleasant recurrence of ‘word,’ unemphasized, three times in four lines, twice at the end of lines spoken by the same character. [DYCE (ed. 2).

57.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Thou fond mad man, hear me a little speak.
notes: fond] COLL. (ed. 1). ‘Fond’ is, of course, here, as in many other places, foolish. [Huds.

58.play: ROMEO: O, thou wilt speak again of banishment.

59.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: I’ll give thee armor to keep off that word,

notes: Adversity’s . . . banished] MAL. So in Romeus and Juliet, the Friar says: 'Vertue is alwayes thrall to troubles and annoye, But wisdome in adversitie findes cause of quiet joye.' See also Lyly’s Euphues, 1580: ‘Thou sayest banishment is better to the freeborne. There be many meates which are sowre in the mouth and sweet in the maw; but if thou mingle them with sweet sawces, they yeeld both a pleasant taste and wholesome nourishment. I speake this to this end; that though thy exile seeme grievous to thee, yet, guiding thyselfe with the rules of philosophy, it shall be more tolerable. [Sing. Hal.

61.play: To comfort thee, though thou art banishèd.

63.play: Unless philosophy can make a Juliet,

64.play: Displant a town, reverse a prince’s doom,

65.play: It helps not, it prevails not. Talk no more.

66.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: O, then I see that madmen have no ears.

67.play: ROMEO: How should they when that wise men have no eyes?

68.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Let me dispute with thee of thy estate.
notes: Let me dispute] STEEV. That is, let me talk over your affairs, or the present state you are in. [SING.] The same phrase, with the same meaning, occurs in The Winter's Tale: IV, iv, 411. STA. Let me reason with you upon your affairs. [Dyce.

69.play: ROMEO: Thou canst not speak of that thou dost not feel.

70.play: Wert thou as young as I, Juliet thy love,

71.play: An hour but married, Tybalt murderèd,

72.play: Doting like me, and like me banishèd,

73.play: Then mightst thou speak, then mightst thou tear thy

75.play: And fall upon the ground as I do now,

77.play: Taking the measure of an unmade grave.

79.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Arise. One knocks. Good Romeo, hide thyself.

80.play: ROMEO: Not I, unless the breath of heartsick groans,

81.play: Mistlike, enfold me from the search of eyes.

83.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Hark, how they knock!—Who’s there?—Romeo,

85.play: Thou wilt be taken.—Stay awhile.—Stand up.
notes: DEL. ('Lex.') Throughout this scene, as well as in the scenes that follow, Sh. represents the readiness of the Friar to act, in contrast to the vain wailings of Romeo and the Nurse. The Friar, therefore, instead of joining in the lamentations of the others, would be much more likely to repress them.

ULR. It is far from being out of character for the Nurse to interlard her talk with some few grand, high-sounding phrases, which she had caught up in her long intercourse with the higher ranks, especially with Lady Capulet. My view is also sustained by the word predicament, which only half and half applies here, inasmuch as it only exceptionally, and under certain circumstances, signifies the same as situation (Lage), and even in this sense does not exactly suit the connection. WHITE. There can hardly be a doubt that Farmer was right.

87.play: Run to my study.—By and by.—God’s will,

88.play: What simpleness is this?—I come, I come.

90.play: Who knocks so hard? Whence come you? What’s

93.play: Let me come in, and you shall know my errand.

98.play: NURSE: O holy friar, O, tell me, holy friar,

99.play: Where’s my lady’s lord? Where’s Romeo?

100.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: There on the ground, with his own tears made

102.play: NURSE: O, he is even in my mistress’ case,

103.play: Just in her case. O woeful sympathy!

104.play: Piteous predicament! Even so lies she,
notes: O woeful...predicament] FARMER. The old copies give these words to the Nurse. One may wonder the edd. did not see that such language must necessarily belong to the Friar.

105.play: Blubb’ring and weeping, weeping and blubb’ring.—

106.play: Stand up, stand up. Stand an you be a man.

107.play: For Juliet’s sake, for her sake, rise and stand.

108.play: Why should you fall into so deep an O?

110.play: NURSE: Ah sir, ah sir, death’s the end of all.

112.play: Spakest thou of Juliet? How is it with her?

113.play: Doth not she think me an old murderer,

114.play: Now I have stained the childhood of our joy

115.play: With blood removed but little from her own?

116.play: Where is she? And how doth she? And what says

117.play: My concealed lady to our canceled love?
notes: conceal’d, &c.] HEATH. The epithet conceal’d is to be understood, not of the person, but of the condition of the lady. So that the sense is, My la’-y, whose being so, together with our marriage which made her so, is concealed from the world. [Sing. Huds. Clarke. WALKER (“Vers.,” p. 291) cites this word as an example under Art. lix. See above, line 13 of this scene.

118.play: NURSE: O, she says nothing, sir, but weeps and weeps,

119.play: And now falls on her bed, and then starts up,

120.play: And “Tybalt” calls, and then on Romeo cries,

123.play: Shot from the deadly level of a gun,

124.play: Did murder her, as that name’s cursèd hand

125.play: Murdered her kinsman.—O, tell me, friar, tell me,

126.play: In what vile part of this anatomy

127.play: Doth my name lodge? Tell me, that I may sack
notes: lodging] ULR. A majority of the edd. prefer 'mansion.' I see not why. DEL. Because it sounds more stately. DYCE. Lodging seems preferable, to say nothing of the word 'mansions' occurring towards the end of this speech. (Compare Petrarch, Canzone v: 'Quando vede 'l pastor calare i raggi Del gran pianeta al nido ov' egli alberga,' &c.) WHITE. 'Mansion' is more ambitious, but less appropriate.

128.play: The hateful mansion. He draws his dagger.

129.play: FRIAR LAWRENCE: Hold thy desperate hand!

130.play: Art thou a man? Thy form cries out thou art.
notes: Art thou a man? &c.] MAL. Sh. has here closely followed Romeus and Juliet: ‘Art thou, quoth he, a man? thy shape saith, so thou art; Thy crying and thy weping eyes denote a woman’s hart,

131.play: Thy tears are womanish; thy wild acts denote

132.play: The unreasonable fury of a beast.
notes: a beast] CLARKE. One of the numerous instances where Sh. uses ‘beast’ as the antithesis to ‘man.’ In As You Like It, IV, iii, 47, Rosalind, disguised as Ganymede, and reading the words: ‘Whiles the eye of man did woo me,’ adds, ‘Meaning me a beast;’ as though that were the necessary sequence in opposition to ‘man.’

133.play: Unseemly woman in a seeming man,

134.play: And ill-beseeming beast in seeming both!

135.play: Thou hast amazed me. By my holy order,

136.play: I thought thy disposition better tempered.

137.play: Hast thou slain Tybalt? Wilt thou slay thyself,

138.play: And slay thy lady that in thy life lives,
notes: And slay, &c.] DEL. This reading is far preferable to that of (Q₁) on account of the accent that falls on the second thy, and because of the Shakspearian collocation of life and lives.

139.play: By doing damnèd hate upon thyself?

140.play: Why railest thou on thy birth, the heaven, and earth,
notes: thy birth] MAL. Romeo has not here railed on his birth, &c., though in his interview with the Friar, as described in the poem, he is made to do so: ‘Fyxt Nature did he blame, the author of his lyfe, In which his joyes had been so scant, and sorowes aye so ryfe; The time and place of byrth he fersly did reprove, He cryed out (with open mouth) against the starres above,— On Fortune eke he raylde.’ Sh. copied the remonstrance of the Friar without reviewing the former part of his scene. He has, in other places, fallen into a similar inaccuracy by sometimes following and sometimes deserting his original. [Sing. Sta. White. ULR. It is true Sh. appears to have followed here the source of his plot a little too closely, but the oversight is not so great as the English critics assume; it can be very readily supposed that before the scene opens Romeo had done what Laurence now reproaches him with. WHITE. The omission in (Q₁) of seventeen lines in this speech is due, without a doubt, to the hasty and surreptitious manner in which that edition was published, and not to the addition of them upon the revision of the play For the supposition for the omission of them on account of too great length, in a play which was shortened throughout, would involve the omission of at least three lines of the close of the speech. That Sh., when, after years of mental development, he revised the early version of this tragedy, began his labour in this passage by finishing a sentence, and then, for the sake of sixteen lines, went helplessly back again to Brooke’s old poem, and, taking it up where he before dropped it, led off by versifying a sentence inconsistent with what he had before written, is too absurd to merit a second thought.

141.play: Since birth and heaven and earth all three do meet

142.play: In thee at once, which thou at once wouldst lose?

143.play: Fie, fie, thou shamest thy shape, thy love, thy wit,

144.play: Which, like a usurer, abound’st in all

145.play: And usest none in that true use indeed

146.play: Which should bedeck thy shape, thy love, thy wit.

147.play: Thy noble shape is but a form of wax,

148.play: Digressing from the valor of a man;
notes: Digressing] BOSWELL. So in Richard II: V, iii, 65. Also in Barnabe Riche’s Farewell: ‘Knowing that you should otherwise have used me than you have, you should have digressed and swarved from your kinde.’ [Sing. Hal. STEEV. So in the 24th book of Homer’s Odyssey, translated by Chapman: ‘— my deservings shall in nought digress From best fame of our race’s foremost merit.’ [Hal.

149.play: Thy dear love sworn but hollow perjury,

150.play: Killing that love which thou hast vowed to cherish;

151.play: Thy wit, that ornament to shape and love,

152.play: Misshapen in the conduct of them both,

153.play: Like powder in a skilless soldier’s flask,
notes: powder] COLL. (ed. 2). We hesitate to alter here, because ‘bower’ is very intelligible and figuratively beautiful in connection with ‘paradise;’ but the (MS.) has, rather prosaically, pour, which, however, was formerly often spelt power.

154.play: Is set afire by thine own ignorance,

155.play: And thou dismembered with thine own defense.
notes: And thou] JOHNSON. And thou torn to pieces with thine own weapons. [Sing. Huds.

156.play: What, rouse thee, man! Thy Juliet is alive,
notes: thy Juliet, &c.] ULR. Here again we must suppose that Romeo, before the opening of this scene, had expressed the fear that Juliet may have been made sick or even killed by horror and pain at his deed.

157.play: For whose dear sake thou wast but lately dead:

158.play: There art thou happy. Tybalt would kill thee,
notes: slew’st…too] slewest Tibalt, there art thou happie Qq. slew’st…happie Ff,

159.play: But thou slewest Tybalt: there art thou happy.

160.play: The law that threatened death becomes thy friend

161.play: And turns it to exile: there art thou happy.

162.play: A pack of blessings light upon thy back;

163.play: Happiness courts thee in her best array;

164.play: But, like a misbehaved and sullen wench,
notes: misbehaved and QqQ, mishaved and Ff, misbehav’d and Theobald.

165.play: Thou pouts upon thy fortune and thy love.
notes: pou't'st upon] KNT. Is to put up used as to put aside?

166.play: Take heed, take heed, for such die miserable.

167.play: Go, get thee to thy love, as was decreed.

168.play: Ascend her chamber. Hence and comfort her.

169.play: But look thou stay not till the watch be set,

170.play: For then thou canst not pass to Mantua,

171.play: Where thou shalt live till we can find a time

172.play: To blaze your marriage, reconcile your friends,

173.play: Beg pardon of the Prince, and call thee back

174.play: With twenty hundred thousand times more joy

175.play: Than thou went’st forth in lamentation.—

176.play: Go before, nurse. Commend me to thy lady,

177.play: And bid her hasten all the house to bed,

178.play: Which heavy sorrow makes them apt unto.

180.play: NURSE: O Lord, I could have stayed here all the night

181.play: To hear good counsel. O, what learning is!—

182.play: My lord, I’ll tell my lady you will come.

183.play: ROMEO: Do so, and bid my sweet prepare to chide.

184.play: NURSE: Here, sir, a ring she bid me give you, sir.
notes: Here, sir] COLL. (ed. 2). The insertion of ‘sir’ twice in the line may have been intended to indicate the state of feeling of the Nurse.
